title: Subquery — find above-average earners
instruction:
A subquery is a SELECT inside another SELECT. The inner query runs first, its result is used by the outer query.

Find all employees earning above the company average salary.
starter:
SELECT
  name,
  job_title,
  salary,
  salary - ROUND((SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)) AS above_avg_by
FROM employees
WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)
ORDER BY salary DESC;
successMessage: 7 of 15 employees earn above average. The subquery `(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)` evaluates to ~$119,362 and is used twice: once in WHERE, once in the computed column. Subqueries make this clean and readable.

title: Subquery with IN — active project departments
instruction:
`IN` with a subquery is a powerful pattern: find all rows that match a list produced by another query.

Find all employees in departments that currently have active projects.
starter:
SELECT DISTINCT
  e.name,
  e.job_title,
  d.name AS department
FROM employees e
JOIN departments d ON e.department_id = d.id
WHERE e.department_id IN (
  SELECT DISTINCT department_id
  FROM projects
  WHERE status = 'active'
)
ORDER BY d.name, e.name;
expectedOutput: Eva Martinez|Head of Data Science|Data Science
successMessage: HR has no active projects, so its 2 employees don't appear. The subquery `SELECT DISTINCT department_id FROM projects WHERE status = 'active'` produces IDs [1, 2, 3, 5], and the outer query filters to only those departments.

title: EXISTS — check for related records
instruction:
`EXISTS` returns true if the subquery produces any rows. It stops as soon as it finds the first match — making it efficient for existence checks.

Find customers who have placed at least one paid order.
starter:
SELECT
  c.name,
  c.country,
  c.joined_date
FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders o
  WHERE o.customer_id = c.id
    AND o.status = 'paid'
)
ORDER BY c.joined_date;
successMessage: 8 customers have at least one paid order. NovaSoft and Vertex Solutions are excluded (only pending orders). `SELECT 1` is conventional — `EXISTS` only cares whether rows exist, not what they contain.
